data.title: St. Joe: Decades Of Value Creation Ahead
data.description:
Introduction

Warren Buffett says that price is what you pay and value is what you get, and I think this phrase applies really well to St. Joe (NYSE:JOE). I don't usually analyze real estate businesses because I don't consider it my strong point so far, but after coming across this company and starting to research it, I believe we might be looking at a very interesting opportunity. The short summary of the thesis is that by buying St. Joe shares, it's like we're buying acres in Florida for 5-10 times less than what they're actually worth. We pay X for acres that are worth 5X-10X, which is why Warren Buffett's famous quote fits so well in this case.

At first glance, St. Joe isn't a company that draws much attention. The stock has been flat for many years and its business requires a lot of patience, something that's not very abundant on Wall Street. However, I think if we analyze the current state of the business, we can see that the years the stock has remained flat have probably meant that a huge gap has opened up between the share price and the real value of the business. In this article, I'll try to explain why.

Bay-Walton Area

Before we go into the specifics of the company's assets, I think it’s almost more important to get a handle on where these assets are actually located. For me, that’s the real key to this whole thing.

St. Joe owns most of its land and properties in northwest Florida, specifically in Bay and Walton counties. As we’ll see, this area is pulling in tons of new residents every year and is growing like crazy, which is huge for St. Joe’s interests. I mean, what’s the point of having thousands of acres if they’re stuck in some desert where nobody wants to live? To be specific, St. Joe’s got about 168,000 acres in this region, and 25% of them are near the coast or the sea, which we’ll talk about in a sec.

This part of the U.S., like pretty much all of Florida, is famous for its awesome weather year-round, especially in winter. While people up north are shivering in below-10-degree Fahrenheit weather for weeks, down here it barely drops below 45, even on the worst days. And since it’s so easy to move around the country these days, the climate’s becoming a bigger deal for folks picking a new place to live. This area’s got a massive edge because of that. There’s an image coming up that shows the average weather in Panama City, one of the big cities around here.

Another thing drawing people to Florida is the low taxes and cheaper cost of living. A bunch of folks are ditching pricey, high-tax states like California and New York for places like Texas or Florida, where living costs and taxes are way lower. Now, if there’s a downside to this spot, it’s that being in Florida means hurricanes are a thing. And honestly, the northwest part of the state is one of the most hurricane-prone areas.

Still, it seems like the good stuff outweighs that risk by a mile, and people are flocking here. Walton and Bay counties have seen insane population growth over the last few decades. In Bay, the population’s doubled since 1980, and in Walton, it’s quadrupled since then.

The average family income in these counties is around $90k in Walton and $80k in Bay (I’m guessing that $8k in the original was a typo!), while the median home price is $376,400 in Walton and $276,900 in Bay. Like we said earlier, housing here is pretty affordable compared to other parts of the U.S. The family income might not be the highest in the country, but it lines up nicely with the cost of living. You can live way better here than in places where salaries are higher, but the cost of living is totally out of control.

Oh, and another big plus? Thanks to its location and killer weather, this area’s a hotspot for tourism, especially for Americans. I couldn’t dig up solid tourist numbers, but we’ve got a decent clue from the Panama City-Bay County airport. That place went from 80,000 passengers a year in 2014 to over 170,000 in 2023—more than doubling in just 10 years. That’s gotta be a good sign that people are getting more and more interested in this region.

The Bay-Walton plan

So, another big thing to know about is the Bay-Walton plan, which got the green light back in 2015. It's basically an extension of the old West Bay Sector Plan. This is a massive, long-term development plan – we're talking a 50-year vision here – that's supposed to guide growth, development, and protect the environment in Bay and Walton counties.

The plan covers about 110,500 acres owned by The St. Joe Company. Most of that land, like 88% of it (that's 97,216 acres), is in Bay County, and the other 12% (13,284 acres) is in Walton County. And get this – over half of that, like 53,000 acres, is set aside for protecting important natural resources.

They're rolling out the plan bit by bit, depending on how the market's doing, through these Detailed Specific Area Plans (DSAPs). Each of these DSAPs has to cover at least 1,000 acres and give the lowdown on land use, infrastructure, nature, and transport.

The plan's got all sorts of development zones – like a Regional Employment Center, Business Center, West Bay Center, Town Center, Village Center, low-impact residential areas, and Long-Term Preservation areas. Plus, they're throwing in over 100 miles of public trails that'll link up with existing regional networks, so you can hike and bike all you want.

Getting this plan approved basically means St. Joe can keep developing land for at least the next 50 years, and it sets up a long-haul strategy for the whole region's growth. St. Joe themselves say they've got the legal go-ahead to build 170,000 homes, 3,000 hotel rooms, and over 22 million square feet of space for businesses and industries.

How do they earn money?

Before valuing St. Joe Company’s assets, it’s essential to understand the developments the company has in operation or in progress. These assets represent a significant portion of its current value. The company structures its operations and generates revenue through three main business segments: Hospitality, Residential, and Commercial.

Hospitality

This segment focuses on capitalizing on the tourist appeal of Northwest Florida, as previously discussed, through the development and management of a portfolio of leisure and accommodation-oriented properties. St. Joe creates and operates hotels, both luxury and more accessible, high-end golf clubs, and marinas. The goal is to attract a broad spectrum of visitors and generate revenue through hotel operations, club memberships, and related services.

Examples of properties include hotels like The Pearl Hotel, WaterColor Inn, and Camp Creek Inn, golf clubs such as Camp Creek and Shark’s Tooth, the exclusive Watersound Club, and marinas like Point South Marina in Port St. Joe. The company has significantly expanded this segment, recently opening several hotels, reaching a total of 12 hotels now in operation. I recommend checking Google Maps for some of the properties mentioned to get a better sense of the area’s appeal and the quality of the properties, though the following image should give you a good idea.

Residential

The residential segment is dedicated to developing various types of housing communities to meet the demand for homes for a growing population in the area. It also manages the strategic sale of developed land, both directly and through joint ventures, moving away from selling large tracts of undeveloped land.

St. Joe develops everything from luxury communities like Watersound Camp Creek to active adult communities (55+) like Latitude Margaritaville Watersound, as well as workforce housing in developments like College Station and Titus Park, and single-family homes in planned communities such as Breakfast Point East and Ward Creek.

Commercial

This segment focuses on creating and managing a diverse portfolio of commercial properties for lease, generating recurring rental income. St. Joe develops properties to meet various commercial, residential, and service needs within its planned communities and the region.

The portfolio includes shopping centers (such as Watersound Town Center and Pier Park North), industrial spaces and business parks, office buildings, retail spaces, apartments (like a 240-unit community in Panama City), senior living facilities, medical centers (such as the FSU/TMH medical campus), and self-storage units. The strategy aims to maintain high occupancy rates to ensure stable revenue streams.

Valuation

Now comes perhaps the most complicated part: valuing all of St. Joe's assets and trying to determine if the price we pay for the company today offers potential or not. First, we will value the developments that are already generating cash flows, and then the remaining land.

Analyzing the three segments reveals spectacular growth across all of them in recent years. There's slightly more fluctuation in the residential segment because land sales aren't evenly distributed each year, somewhat distorting the figures. Even so, the growth is clearly excellent in all three areas.

Taking into account the operating margins and general and administrative expenses, we arrive at a value for the company's operating businesses of about $1.8 billion. It should be noted that the multiples used are, in my opinion, very conservative. Some segments could easily be valued at more than 15x EBIT given the recurrence and predictability of these cash flows, but as I mentioned, I prefer to be extremely conservative to leave a good margin of safety.

Next, for valuing the undeveloped land, I've decided to divide the acreage into 4 groups, as clearly not all the company's land holds the same value. First, to the 53,000 acres protected by the Bay-Walton Sector Plan, I have assigned a value of $5k per acre. This value seems extremely conservative to me, as the average acre in Florida is worth around $33-36k, but given that there won't be major developments on this land, I prefer to assign them minimal value.

Then, I decided to divide the remaining acres into 3 groups: high, medium, and low quality. An acre on the coast can be worth over $250k, and there have even been transactions for over $1M. So, I have assumed that 10% of the remaining acres are of very high quality, meaning located by the sea, lakes, or important cities. Then 40%, I have valued as medium-quality acres, meaning near the prime quality ones but somewhat further from important areas or close to roads or industries. To these acres, I have assigned $40k each, slightly above the state average but well below the value of acres in similar areas. The rest I have valued at $15k per acre, less than half the state average, as they are in less desirable areas for residential or commercial development

Therefore, being extremely conservative with the price per acre, we arrive at a value that could currently exceed $4 billion. It is also important to consider that inflation and the fact that it is an in-demand area, as we have seen, will increase the value of these acres annually.

Adding the value of both parts and subtracting the debt, we arrive at a value for St. Joe of around $5.5 billion, although its current market capitalization is about $2.5 billion. Therefore, by valuing both its operating part and future developments very conservatively, we obtain a valuation double the current one. In fact, if we subtract the $1.7Bn from the operating business value, we can say that by buying St. Joe, we are acquiring 168,000 acres in one of Florida's most attractive areas for about $4,500 per acre, which is nearly 10 times less than the state average.

Perhaps, the only thing required for this value to be realized is patience. The developments will gradually occur, and the cash flows will arrive, but we must be more patient than the market. Currently, they generate about $107M in FFO, which means the company trades at about 22x FFO. Therefore, buying St. Joe means acquiring commercial, residential, and hospitality developments in a very high-demand area of Florida, at a quite reasonable price, and with what seems like an underappreciation of the company's future potential. For me, this company is a clear "Strong Buy" if you have enough patience for the developments to be completed.

data.title: Gladstone Commercial: A Great 9% Yielding Industrial REIT To Buy
data.description:
Gladstone Commercial Corporation (NASDAQ:GOOD) is a well-managed real estate investment trust that focuses primarily on the ownership and operation of an industrial-centric property portfolio.

Though Gladstone Commercial is predominantly focused on industrial net lease properties, which accounted for about 2/3rds of the REIT’s portfolio income as of December 31, 2024, the real estate investment trust owned a considerable amount of office properties as well.

Since investors recently soured on owning stocks in general, I think that the correction we have seen in the market as of late opens up an opportunity to buy Gladstone Commercial’s stock. Previously, in my piece titled ‘Gladstone Capital: 9% Yield, Improving Coverage, 1.12x NAV’, I highlighted positive trends in the eCommerce market specifically as being supportive of my bullish investment thesis.

I think that Gladstone Commercial will be able to pay its $0.30 per share per quarter dividend all throughout 2025, and I see a positive risk/reward relationship as the portfolio tilts further toward industrial properties.

Industrial-Centric Real Estate Portfolio

Gladstone Commercial has pursued a strategy of growing its industrial real estate portfolio in the last couple of years in a major attempt to shift its portfolio toward high-growth real estate assets. As the commercial real estate trust boosted its presence in the industrial real estate market, it simultaneously scaled back its office exposure, making Gladstone Commercial more and more of an industrial-centric real estate investment.

As of December 31, 2024, Gladstone Commercial’s industrial assets made up 63% of total income derived from its properties. Offices, now relegated to a distant number 2 in terms of income contribution, accounted for just 33% of portfolio income in the December-quarter.

Gladstone Commercial also owned smaller percentages of its portfolio invested in retail and medical offices, but these assets don’t make a substantial income contribution.

Gladstone Commercial has aggressively doubled down on its investments in industrial real estate in recent years, a move that was backed by the notion that a growing eCommerce market would require more investments in industrial real estate infrastructure, such as warehouses. In 2024, 63% of income in Gladstone Commercial’s real estate portfolio was produced by industrial real estate, up from 35% in 2018.

Gladstone Commercial also has a very well-occupied real estate portfolio that ensures the payment of a steady amount of funds from operations. In 2024, Gladstone Commercial’s occupancy was 98.7%, up 0.9 percentage points YoY.

Gladstone Commercial earned $0.35 per share in core funds from operations in the fourth quarter, reflecting a decrease of 2.8% YoY. The real estate investment trust’s dividend pay-out ratio in 4Q24 as 86% compared to 79% in 3Q24.

The dividend, as far as coverage is concerned, is quite well-covered, though Gladstone Commercial has the highest dividend pay-out ratio in the peer group of industrial REITs.

Gladstone Commercial is primarily focused on industrial REITs, and so are Broadstone Net Lease, Inc. (BNL), STAG Industrial, Inc. (STAG) and W. P. Carey (WPC) whose LTM dividend pay-out ratios move between 61% to 84%, with the peer group average being 74%.

Gladstone Commercial had the highest LTM dividend pay-out ratio compared to its industrial REIT peers in 2024, but I do not think that investors need to be concerned about the dividend given the relative stability of its core funds from operations.

Industrial Real Estate Tailwinds

The eCommerce industry is anticipated to remain a booming industry in the coming years, which implies that warehouses, distribution centers and other industrial real estate properties should remain in high demand.

Growing eCommerce penetration rates, expected to grow from 22.5% in 2024 to 29.9% by 2028, hint at growing parcel volumes, which in turn should be a tailwind for industrial property utilization.

I think with eCommerce growth being a main driver of the industrial real estate industry, the prospects for rental growth and occupancy for REITs like Gladstone Commercial are leaning clearly on the positive side. Based on Moody’s information, industrial rents kept going up in 4Q24, reflecting 0.3% YoY growth.

Low FFO Multiple Reflects A High Margin Of Safety

Gladstone Commercial is a bargain based on 4Q24 run-rate core funds from operations. The real estate investment trust, based off of funds from operations, is the cheapest industrial REIT investment in the peer group as its stock is 14% cheaper than the average industrial real estate investment trust.

Gladstone Commercial is selling for 9.6x 4Q24 annualized core funds from operations, whereas Broadstone Net Lease is selling for a core FFO multiple of 9.9x. Both STAG Industrial and W.P. Carey have considerably higher Price-to-FFO ratios, ranging between 12-13x FFO.

STAG Industrial is a unique REIT in the peer group because it is 100% focused on industrial real estate and is a solid REIT investment if you desire steady, monthly dividend income. Since STAG Industrial is also focused fully on the industrial real estate market, it is the only pure play industrial REIT in the peer group here.

W.P. Carey is also focused on warehouses and industrial properties as a main source of producing rental income, but the trust retains a big position (22%) of retail investments (in the United States and abroad), so the real estate investment trust offers investors a much better real estate portfolio. The price for this better diversification, however, is a higher FFO multiple.

In my view, Gladstone Commercial should be able to re-rate to a 10-11x funds from operations multiple as the portfolio is performing well, producing predictable core FFO and has robust portfolio lease metrics, and I anticipate the real estate investment trust to prioritize growth in the fast-growing industrial real estate class moving forward. Thus, the trust’s implied intrinsic value is $15.40 at the high point, and $14.00 at the low point.

Why The Investment Thesis Might Turn Out To Be More Bearish

Gladstone Commercial is increasingly oriented toward industrial real estate, which makes the trust more of a concentrated bet on this particular real estate asset class.

If Gladstone Commercial is going to continue to overweight industrial real estate, the REIT is obviously going to be more vulnerable to a possible downturn in the eCommerce industry and could potentially miss out on higher returns in other markets, such as U.S. offices.

My Conclusion

Passive income vehicles such as Gladstone Commercial have become considerably more compelling as income investments after the recent market carnage.

In my view, the overweighting of industrial real estate has made strategic sense for Gladstone Commercial as the real estate investment trust’s industrial real estate assets profoundly profit from growing eCommerce transactions and a broadening real estate infrastructure to support the growth of the industry.

Though Gladstone Commercial has the highest core FFO-based dividend pay-out ratio in the peer group, I think that the real estate investment trust offers passive income investors a well-covered dividend and should be in a good position to maintain its $0.30 per share per quarter dividend pay-out.

Moreover, the fundamentals in the industrial real estate market are looking robust, underscoring that Gladstone Commercial’s strategic shift into industrial properties is paying off.

data.title: Pfizer: Risks Priced In, Buy The Exceptional Valuation And Yield
data.description:
Pfizer Inc (NYSE:PFE) has had a troublesome few years after the revenue boom generated by its COVID vaccine. The company was among the few beneficiaries of the need to rapidly vaccinate massive swaths of the global population, competing chiefly against Moderna (MRNA) and Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) in the Western world. The rapid ascent of the company's revenue and EPS resulted in several months of exceptional stock returns. The success was short-lived, though. Once COVID infection rates normalized and have now hit a 'steady-state' flu-like infection cycle, Pfizer's once ludicrous sales growth quickly became overcome with burdensome internal bloat and looming patent cliffs. Several billions in revenue related to currently protected drugs are at risk as the company will face margin compression from generic drug proliferation. The assets at risk include Prevnar (2026), Ibrance (2027), Xtandi (2027) and Eliquis (2028). Further, the company's upcoming product pipeline recently took a hit as it announced that it will be halting the development of its GLP-1 weight loss drug. Deterioration in the company's total revenue, net margin, and growth prospects have created the perfect storm of headwinds for the stock, leading to abysmal performance:

Despite the horrible recent returns, Pfizer remains a key player in the global pharmaceutical industry, touts a very attractive gross margin (suggesting continued pricing power), has undergone a series of cost-cutting measures, and maintains a robust R&D pipeline bolstered by recent acquisitions. Adding to this, the company recently increased its dividend, now eclipsing a mouth-watering 7% yield. In this article, I'll discuss the nature of the pharmaceutical industry, Pfizer's plan to rise from the post-COVID ashes, and why I believe there's too much pessimism baked into the stock.

Big Pharma: Big R&D, Big Markups, and Patent Cliffs

The pharmaceutical industry has become prolific in the US as the United States people have become the most medicated population in the world. America's obsession with prescription medication is illustrated best in the recent craze over GLP-1 medications, which were initially intended as type-2 diabetes medications but found an enormous market in 'treating' obesity. The paradox here is clear: Americans want to take a pill to 'cure' obesity so that they don't need to make the requisite lifestyle changes to achieve this. This recent study found that patients without type-2 diabetes exhibited higher discontinuation rates after 1 year and lower reinitiation rates than those with type-2 diabetes. This suggests that these patients used GLP-1 drugs specifically to lose weight, stopped once they achieved some arbitrary weight loss goal, and didn't return to use. However, without changing their lifestyle, it's likely that these folks will face a high recidivism rate for obesity. It is Americans' desire for a quick, easy fix to life's ills that has driven the pharma industry to become enormous. This type of problematic behavior has led to the rise of a counterculture, typified by the current FDA chair Robert F. Kennedy, an outspoken adversary to vaccines.

The pharmaceutical industry itself has had a part to play in this. America is among the few countries globally that allows widespread marketing efforts for prescription drugs. The logic makes sense at a base level: big pharma companies have massive research expenditures to discover new drugs, so they need mechanisms to monetize successful discoveries to reinvest in future research. The ability to freely advertise new patent-protected medications priced at an enormous markup is the 'mechanism' in question. Over time patent protections expire, allowing generic or biosimilar drugs to saturate the market and drive costs down for consumers. This is good for consumers but decimates the previous monopolists' margin on a given drug. This necessitates aggressive investment in R&D to maintain a robust portfolio of high-margin, patented drugs.

This system should lead to more novel drug discoveries that can treat more diseases and improve aggregate health outcomes over time. The reality is much more nuanced, as I previously noted the bleak side of the American obsession with pharmaceutical drugs. I don't intend to debate the ethics of the current state of pharmacy in the US, rather I am making the claim that it's a system which I don't see changing overnight. From an investor's perspective, this makes big pharma companies a very attractive long-term investment opportunity.

Given that context, the most important analysis for a pharmaceutical stock is not of its current portfolio but of its development pipeline.

Pfizer's R&D Pipeline, Bolstered by Acquisitions

Pfizer management is not blind to the bear case of the stock. Patent cliffs are an existential threat to any Big Pharma company and a reality that they've all had to grapple with. Those that remain dominant today, Pfizer included, are well-versed in these types of scenarios. That's why the company announced aggressive cost-cutting measures post-COVID and throughout 2027 aiming to eliminate $5b in costs. While corporate rightsizing has very real effects on employee morale, a return to profitability can quickly re-invigorate employees who have significant stock-based compensation. The company rewarded employees with $877m in stock-based comp in 2024 alone, ensuring the remaining employees have the incentive to keep the R&D engine pumping.

To its credit and despite analyst negativity, Pfizer maintains a pipeline of 100+ drugs in development, with a particular focus on Oncology and Biosimilar treatments. Much of the Oncology pipeline was acquired in the recent Seagen acquisition, after which the CEO Albert Bourla commented, “We believe Oncology will be a significant growth driver for Pfizer and contribute meaningfully to the achievement of our near- and long-term financial goals”.

The Seagen acquisition doubled Pfizer's Oncology pipeline, now at 59, with 8 blockbuster drugs now expected by 2030. The company also recently launched an RSV vaccine named Abrysvo and an ulcerative colitis treatment called Velsipity. The company expects these to be material contributors to sales by 2030. Further, Pfizer is working on a joint flu/COVID vaccine that could generate meaningful annual revenues during flu/COVID season and offers better unit economics than providing a vaccine for either of them individually.

Overall, Pfizer's product pipeline and strategic initiatives exist in stark contrast to beaten down market sentiment. The company is not some fledgling biotech dependent on one blockbuster drug to catapult its profits. It is a stalwart of the American pharmaceutical industry that is not going to zero. While recent sales growth has been abysmal, the company offers an exceptionally high yield and an extremely attractive valuation considering the product pipeline. Current valuation metrics suggest very limited business potential, while reality exists in stark contrast to this.

Financials

Pfizer currently trades at around a $125b market cap, about 2x 2024 sales of $63b. The company enjoys a prosperous 74% gross margin in 2024, up 4 percentage points from 2023. Despite this, 2024 results still present risks. Most notably is the $67b in total debt, resulting in a $3b interest expense in 2024. The company's $2.2b net interest expense was roughly 12.5% of 2024 operating income, so deleveraging is a key priority moving forward.

Besides that, I see the balance of patent cliffs and upcoming blockbuster releases as broadly offsetting each other from a margin perspective. The company will need to rely on sales growth to generate EPS growth and significant stock returns. However, investors today are more than reimbursed for taking on the risks inherent, as the stock costs about $22.50 and pays an annual dividend of $1.72. This translates to a yield in excess of 7.5% and a quarterly dividend that was recently increased from $0.42 to $0.43. The safest dividend is that one that was just increased. Management and the board are still clearly confident in Pfizer's long-term ability to generate profitable growth. I broadly agree with management, and therefore believe right now is an exceptional opportunity to initiate a position in Pfizer.

Investor Takeaway

Pfizer is a titan of the pharmaceutical industry that has faced several material headwinds in recent years. However, the current bearish narrative is more driven by stock returns than fundamental issues with the company. For this reason, I'm initiating my coverage of Pfizer stock with a Strong Buy on the very compelling 7x PE and exceptional dividend yield.

data.title: OPTZ: Not The Best Investment In A Tariff Sensitive Market
data.description:
As many investors in my place would be, I am usually very skeptical of ETFs that follow a passive investment strategy but nevertheless charge 'active rates' - and then struggle to deliver ongoing alpha. On the surface, Optimize Strategy Index ETF (NASDAQ:OPTZ) seems to be such a fund: the selection criteria are mostly quantitative and very specific, which makes it easier to manage; a higher-than-normal expense ratio for a passive fund, which I find concerning; and, finally, a performance profile that is unimpressive, at best.

OPTZ: Purpose and Strategy

The purpose of the fund is clearly spelled out:

...allows individual investors to participate in the long-term growth of innovative American companies. Over many decades, the U.S. stock market has rewarded those who have shown unyielding faith in the power of successful companies to grow shareholder value.

One might find it intriguing that an ETF would use profound phrases like "unyielding faith" to promote itself and advance its agenda, but that intrigue only lasts until one understands the filtering criteria for its cache of 306 equity holdings. These criteria essentially answer the question "how do you pick innovative, successful, American companies that will grow shareholder value over a long time-window?"

This is how the ETF makes its picks, as I understand it:

  1. The first step is to assess specific fundamentals - current earnings per share, ten-year average EPS, two years' forward growth for EPS, and price to sales data
  2. Having graded these metrics using what I can reasonably assume is a proprietary quantitative model, the equity universe used for the selection is then divided into two groups - one on either side of the $2 billion market capitalization mark. Note that P/S is used for the sub-$2-billion group in addition to the other metrics
  3. The next step is the sector-wise grouping, following which the top 20 percent in each sector under each market cap grouping is selected for the fund to invest in.

The fund rebalances periodically, and since it celebrates its first birthday on April 22, we can use the 39 percent annualized turnover as an accurate yardstick for the intensity and aggressiveness of these rebalances.

I will say that I find it a little on the high side, but corporate earnings were volatile over 2024 so it is reasonable to expect a quant model to have kept making adjustments throughout the four quarters.

This is also why I find the 0.50 percent expense ratio to be unusually high. Passive funds tend to have fewer transactions and lower management fees, as is the case with ETFs like SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) offering 0.09 percent or even Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ) at a higher 0.20 percent. Why should I pay half a percentage of my total return in exchange for a passive portfolio that is algorithmically chosen? That is the issue I have with this fund.

Alright, so the basics of the fund do not add up for me, but how effective has its strategy been in the past year since inception? We briefly saw that it was unimpressive, but it deserves a closer look in terms of where it deviated from the performance of its benchmark, S&P 500 Index (SP500).

The OPTZ Disadvantage

On a total return basis, the fund does not seem to have performed that badly. It did not beat the market, no, but it does seem to have kept ahead of the index and its total returns for much of its existence. Not coincidentally, at least in my view of things, this outperformance received a proper trouncing just weeks after Trump took office. This points to an inference I cannot ignore - the market suddenly decided to take a ride on the rollercoaster, punishing growth and value equities alike, indiscriminate in its slashing of market value across the spectrum. The tariff catalyst, whether it materializes or not, has been a most fearsome one for market participants.

That makes this an inappropriate time to invest in equities because the fund's diversified holdings seem to do very little to protect investors on the downside.

There no longer appears to be any value assigned to value, to put it bluntly.

Strong fundamentals, however, tend to bring an equity back to its fair value much more quickly once the market gains more visibility into the future. When that might happen is anybody's guess.

On the positive side of it, we know that the ETF did outperform SP500 in a relatively bullish market leading up to the disruption, so it does generate alpha returns when market sentiment is overall positive.

This is your cue. What you must decide, as an investor, is whether you possess the patience to keep your funds in relatively liquid assets (they pay a fair yield, you know) so there is adequate dry powder to acquire valuable equities at fire-sale prices if and when a recession hits - or, if you are brave enough to buy those same equities now via an ETF like OPTZ, which has the potential to outperform the market and possibly spring back healthier than before once the tariff winds subside.

The second approach obviously entails a much higher level of risk because we have no idea if a major drawdown is still on the cards for the broader market. The eyes and ears of the world are focused on the painfully piecemeal and abrupt delivery of the president's 'Trump-start' announcements on tariffs, so any major news item that breaks is likely to influence the market one way or another. I believe they call it gambling.

This is my reason for assigning a hold to OPTZ. It outperforms more than marginally when market sentiment is overall bullish, but takes an ugly turn during market shocks... and the market is extremely shock-sensitive right now. What the ETF says on its website seems true: this is only for investors with "unyielding faith in the power of successful companies to grow shareholder value". For ye of little faith, this is a Hold.

data.title: Western Asset GSM 5-Year Portfolios Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Review

  • Economic uncertainties triggered a flight to quality during the first quarter of 2025, driving U.S. Treasury yields lower and their prices higher. The economy continued to expand, while inflation remained higher than the Federal Reserve’s (“Fed”) 2% target. Meanwhile, President Trump’s plans for new reciprocal tariffs on U.S. imports led to concerns of rising inflation. All told, the overall U.S. bond market, as measured by the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Index, returned 2.78% during the first quarter. In contrast, the municipal (“muni”) market modestly declined over the quarter.

Quarterly Key Performance Drivers

Allocation

Security Selection

HELPED

Yield curve positioning

HURT

Duration positioning
  • Having a duration that was slightly higher or neutral versus the benchmark detracted from returns.
  • Yield curve positioning was beneficial, as the curve began to steepen.
  • Sector allocation and security selection did not meaningfully impact the strategy’s relative performance during the quarter.

Outlook & Strategy

  • The disinflationary trend may be interrupted as tariffs and retaliatory actions are implemented, but we expect inflation to move lower again over the longer term. Monetary policy remains restrictive. We expect central banks will continue to cut rates modestly in 2025. The Fed remains well positioned to provide support if the U.S. economy falters. Public debt levels continue to rise and yield curves may steepen given concerns over fiscal policies globally.
  • We see pockets of opportunity in developed market rates in Europe, the UK and Australia. While the overall uncertainty in the market environment necessitates caution, we do see some longer-term value opportunities in emerging market local currency debt.
  • Spread sector fundamentals remain supportive, but valuations reflect those fundamentals and credit spreads persist at below historical averages. We continue to find opportunities within spread sectors and related securities, while remaining tactical.
  • Developed market duration provides useful diversification.

data.title: Northern Dynasty Minerals: A Glimmer Of Hope For Pebble, But Many Hurdles Remain
data.description:
Northern Dynasty Minerals (NYSE:NAK) was given a glimmer of hope with President Trump's election and his interest in boosting domestic mineral production. However, there are still many hurdles to Pebble becoming an actual working mine.

Pebble ran into permitting issues late in President Trump's first term, several months after Donald Trump Jr. expressed opposition to it. While the permitting situation could be reversed in Trump's second term, I'd still put the chances of Pebble getting approval as being relatively low (albeit more than the zero chance it had under the Biden administration).

The project economics have improved with strong gold and copper prices, and I'd estimate its NPV10 (based on its 2023 PEA) at around $4 billion to $4.5 billion at current metals prices.

That is a positive, but there would still be challenges in finding project financing (and/or a large partner) given the nearly $6.8 billion initial capex estimate.

I believe that Northern Dynasty's shares are pricing in too high a chance that Pebble will receive approval under the second Trump administration, although at least the odds aren't zero now.

This report uses US dollars unless otherwise noted and an exchange rate of US$1.00 to CAD$1.40.

Current Status

Northern Dynasty noted in mid-February that it had consented to the EPA's request for a 90-day abeyance with regards to the ongoing litigation. This is to give the new administration time to consider how it wants to proceed with the case. Thus in mid-May there should be more information on whether the administration's position on Pebble has changed.

Either way though, I'd expect litigation to continue for many years. There is also the risk that a favorable result for Pebble under the current administration could get reversed with the next administration (if there is a Democratic President).

Notes On Cash And Shares

Northern Dynasty reported having 538.6 million outstanding shares with its 2024 report. It also has 8.4 million in warrants with an exercise price of CAD$0.45 (US$0.32) per share and 11.2 million options that are currently in-the-money at an exercise price of CAD$0.41 (US$0.29) per share.

As well Northern Dynasty has US$15 million in convertible notes that could convert into 42.2 million shares and 1.7 million in deferred or restricted stock units.

Adding all those together, Northern Dynasty could have approximately 602 million in outstanding shares.

Northern Dynasty also reported having CAD$16.1 million (US$11.5 million) in available cash at the end of 2024. This is close to one year's worth of operational cash burn for Northern Dynasty (based on 2024).

Exercise of those options and warrants could add another CAD$8.4 million (US$6 million) to Northern Dynasty's cash position, while there up to US$36 million in payments that Northern Dynasty may receive if the royalty holder exercises its right to purchase additional gold and silver production rights at Pebble. The royalty holder has until July 26, 2025 to exercise its purchase rights.

Northern Dynasty is in reasonable shape with its current cash position, with enough cash to last until mid-2026 if the warrants and options are exercised. If the royalty holder exercises its purchase rights, that would be a further boost, covering around one year of operational cash burn for each US$12 million tranche that is purchased.

Notes On Valuation

Commodity prices have significantly increased compared to the prices Northern Dynasty used in its 2023 PEA for Pebble. That had a base case involving $3.90 copper and $1,700 gold, resulting in a post-tax NPV7 of around $2.2 billion at those prices.

Adjusting the 2023 PEA for current prices of $3,350 gold and $4.70 copper increases the post-tax NPV7 to around $6.7 billion.

With the 30-year treasury yield at around 4.7% though, a 7% discount rate seems too low. Using a 10% discount rate would significantly reduce the NPV, perhaps to around $4 billion to $4.5 billion.

The prices for other metals (such as molybdenum) have also improved, but copper and gold make up close to 90% of the value of Pebble's production. There is also the potential for increased costs (compared to the 2023 PEA) to reduce the value of Pebble.

If Pebble was fully guaranteed to go ahead, I could see Northern Dynasty having an intrinsic value of US$2 per share. This would be around 0.3x NPV10 for the full project, with Northern Dynasty likely needing to give up a large stake in Pebble in any sort of partnership with a larger company.

I'd still consider Pebble's odds as fairly low though, so I'd discount its intrinsic value to around US$0.40 to US$0.50 though.

That being said, I can see Northern Dynasty's share price getting to US$2+ if the EPA reversed its veto. That would reflect the positive sentiment before the realities of future court battles and project financing came into play.

Conclusion

Northern Dynasty has received some renewed hope with the change in administrations and the interest in increasing domestic production of minerals. That being said, Pebble was also denied permits under the first Trump administration, so I believe that Pebble is more likely than not to remain stalled.

I believe that Northern Dynasty's stock is pricing in too high of a chance that it will get the go-ahead with the second Trump administration, although its odds have improved from the zero chance under the Biden administration.

data.title: Altria: Delivering BIG Dividends Now And Into The Future
data.description:
One of the most successful stocks of all time is Altria (NYSE:MO). A single dollar invested back in 1968 would've turned into $6,638. With dividends reinvested, a casual return of 663,700% (Over 20% annually). Now, I know what you're thinking-

"That's backwards looking, we want to find stocks that can provide us returns in the future."

And you are exactly right. However, when it comes to Altria, there are some 2 main concerns that investors have that we certainly have to address:

  1. Altria's dividend is not sustainable
  2. Altria is a dying business

These are the 2 most common critiques of Altria that I often see. However, if we look closely at the underlying fundamentals, at what management has told us about their capital allocation plan, and the key competitive advantage this company has, we'll find that these critiques don't seem to hold up.

"Altria's Dividend is not Sustainable"

Let's start with the dividend. For most of the past few years, Altria's dividend yield has been in the range of 8% to 9%.

However, after a 37% increase in the share price over the past year, the current dividend yield is now sitting at around 7.20%.

There's no doubt, everyone likes a high-yielding dividend stock. But the reality for many high-yielding stocks is they struggle to grow their dividend payments over time, some don't grow them at all, and in worse case scenarios, they cut the dividend altogether. For the past few years, I've frequently seen investors state the dividend payouts from Altria are not sustainable. I believe much of this sentiment comes from the fact that the dividend yield from 2015 to 2017 was in the 3% to 4% range. Often times, a rapidly climbing dividend yield might indicate that a dividend is unsustainable (I'm looking at you, AT&T). But the reality is that a change in share price means nothing about the sustainability of the dividend, it's about the change in cash flows. With that being said, if cash flows decrease, the share price is likely to decrease as well. So what has been happening lately with Altria's cash flows?

Since 2018, Altria's free cash flow has covered their total dividends paid. The 5-year free cash flow compounded annual growth rate is 2.55%. Not fast, but certainly growing. With that being said, their free cash flow payout ratio is currently sitting at 79.49%. For most investors, that payout ratio is a bit higher than we would want to see, and could potentially be a red flag. But this is why it's so important we understand management's capital allocation plan. According to a statement on Altria's website-

Altria maintains its long-term objective of a dividend payout ratio target of approximately 80% of its adjusted diluted EPS.

So an 80% payout ratio is not at all a red flag for Altria, it means their payout ratio is completely in line with the management team's stated goals. On top of this, Altria has stated in their recent annual report that they expect to deliver full-year 2025 adjusted diluted EPS of $5.22 to $5.37. This range represents an adjusted diluted EPS growth rate of 2% to 5%. This will not only help Altria maintain their large dividend payments, but will allow them to continue their 50+ year streak of rewarding shareholders with growing dividend payments.

"Altria is a Dying Business"

It's easy to see why investors have concerns about the Altria business model. Smoking rates have declined significantly over the decades according to the American Lung Association, going from 42.4% of adults in 1965, to just 11.6% in 2022.

The end result of this has been a revenue compounded annual growth rate over the past decade of just 1.31%.

Seeing such slow top line growth is a bit alarming, but there is more to the story. Altria is a company that has a serious competitive advantage with its pricing power. Over the past decade, their gross profit ratio has gone from 56.62%, to 70.27%.

Many investors don't seem to realize this, but a competitive advantage possessed by a company often shows up in the underlying fundamentals of a company in 2 ways:

  1. Gross Profit Ratio continues to climb higher
  2. Return on invested capital is consistently above 20%

We've already seen that their pricing power has contributed to a rapidly growing gross profit ratio. But what about return on invested capital? Here is what Altria's ROIC has looked like for the past decade:

Year 2020 2021 2022 2023 2024
ROIC 17.64% 23.35% 22.26% 30.28% 33.20%

So, the projects they reinvest their capital into have proven to be quite profitable. To be fair, we do have to keep in mind that the majority of their earnings/cash flow is being used to pay out dividends, so their reinvestment rate back into the business is low. However, it should still give us confidence that management has a successful track record of allocating capital in ways that create immense shareholder value.

In the short term, Altria has enough pricing power and return on invested capital is high enough to continue growing earnings and free cash flow in the short term. However, history tells us that sales growth is the key driver of long term stock performance.

And this is exactly why Altria's management has been pivoting. Their first page of their recent annual report states how they are seeking to adapt to a smoke-free future. Revenue from their 'smoke-free' categories continues to grow.

This trend should continue, as management has also stated that their planned area of investment will be marketplace activities in support of smoke-free products, as well as research, development, and regulatory preparations of smoke-free products.

Valuation

Despite a 37% increase in the share price over the past year, there still appears to be some upside based on the current valuation for Altria. Using a discounted cash flow analysis, if we assume a free cash flow growth rate of just 2% over the next decade, we still come to a fair value of $65.34. Analysts are currently projecting 3.87% earnings growth over the next 3 to 5 years, so 2% free cash flow growth should be well in reach.

If we look at Altria from a dividend discount model perspective, we see a similar result. Dividend growth over the long term follows earnings/free cash flow growth, and management is targeting a payout ratio of 80%. So again, if we assume a dividend growth rate of just 2% moving forward, we come to a fair value of $63.40.

Keep in mind, management has stated that they are targeting an 80% payout ratio, and are also targeting an adjusted diluted EPS growth rate of 2% to 5%, meaning we will likely see dividend growth within that range as well.

Risks

The key risk to Altria is discussed in their form 10-k. Cigarette volumes continued to fall in 2024. As we've already discussed, Altria's immense pricing power has been able to mitigate this problem so far. But there is a limit to how effective pricing power can be in the long term when volume is decreasing. Along with this, if the growth of illicit products continues to trend upward (as it has in the past few years), this could further erode Altria's pricing power.

“Recent enforcement actions... have not had a material impact in curbing the proliferation and sale of illicit disposable e-vapor products.”

Altria is actively petitioning congress for increased enforcement against illegal disposable e-cigarettes.

Conclusion

While the headlines may paint Altria as a company in decline, the fundamentals, projections, and moves management is making tell a different story. Smoking rates are declining, but Altria has continued to increase their earnings despite low revenue growth due to their incredible pricing power. Their dividend payments appear to be much more sustainable than many realize, the company is continuing to generate high returns on invested capital, and they are investing their capital in areas to create revenue streams in a smoke-free future. On top of all this, the company still appears to be trading at a solid valuation. For investors looking for high-yield options, this should certainly still be on your watchlist.

data.title: Amazon Downgrade: Forget Tariffs And Recession, There's Bigger Risk That Investors Must Consider
data.description:
Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) stock is down around 25% from its all-time high just two months ago, as it is one of those companies that is greatly impacted by the Trump administration’s global trade war. While the bulls may argue that this is a transitory issue, there is also another long-term risk to Amazon’s e-commerce dominance. Rising AI applications like ChatGPT and Perplexity are also expanding into facilitating more commerce activities, threatening to direct more traffic to non-Amazon retail websites. AMZN is being downgraded to a ‘hold’ rating.

In the previous article, we discussed Amazon’s leadership position in robotics, and how it could benefit both its e-commerce and cloud computing business, given the number of AWS services aimed at robotics development. We covered how the growing use of robotics within just Amazon’s U.S. facilities could result in a 15% lift to company-wide operating margins by 2030.

However, the global trade war has been intensifying since then, particularly with U.S. tariffs on goods from China now totaling 145% (barring the exemption of electronics recently).

Many sellers on Amazon count on China for manufacturing and assembly due to lower costs and established infrastructure – up to 70% of goods on Amazon come from China, according to Wedbush Securities.

…

Amazon CEO Andy Jassy … told CNBC that its vast network of third-party sellers will likely “pass the cost on” to consumers.

- CNBC (emphasis added)

Higher prices for such goods sold on the Amazon marketplace will inevitably lead to consumers cutting back on expenditure, undermining revenue growth prospects this year.

For Amazon’s own private label goods sold on the marketplace, the company is expected to mostly absorb the tariffs instead of passing them onto consumers, which would lead to profit margin compression for the e-commerce giant.

But the trade war and increasing likelihood of a recession is not the only headwind Amazon faces at the moment.

The ongoing generative AI revolution continues to transform digital shopping trends and behaviors, with consumers increasingly using alternatives to Amazon’s marketplace to start their product searches.

Amazon faces growing competition from AI-powered alternatives

The rise of AI applications like ChatGPT and Claude had already been transforming the way people searched for information. Inevitably, it has also been influencing the way people research various types of products before making purchase decisions.

According to a recent survey conducted by Adobe Analytics based on 1 trillion visits to U.S. retail sites, … traffic to retail sites from AI-driven searches increased 1,200% in February compared to July 2024 and has doubled every two months since September 2024.

“This is reshaping how businesses think about customer engagement, especially with the arrival of AI agents that will be able to handle more complex tasks and make highly tailored recommendations,” Adobe Digital Insights lead analyst Vivek Pandya said.

– Seeking Alpha News

Indeed, generative AI-powered Search engines (e.g. Google’s AI Overviews and Perplexity) and chat assistants (e.g. ChatGPT, Gemini, etc.) are becoming increasingly important sources of consumer traffic for online webstores.

In fact, OpenAI revealed earlier this month that it had reached a milestone of 500 million Weekly Active Users (WAUs), up from 300 million WAUs in December 2024, reflecting rapid consumer adoption of the chat assistant for various different use cases.

For context, Amazon’s marketplace had an average of 454.6 million monthly unique visitors in January 2025.

Moreover, web analytics platform ‘Similarweb’ recently publicized daily traffic numbers for the most frequently used chat assistants on the market.

ChatGPT remains the king of chatbots, with 146 million daily visits. Moreover, OpenAI has been making some significant moves over the past several months to facilitate more commercial activities. In October 2024, the company introduced its own Search engine “ChatGPT Search” with separate search verticals such as “Maps”.

Furthermore, OpenAI has also disclosed ambitions to launch its own web browser soon, and has already introduced its own AI agent that can conduct tasks on behalf of users within browsers, called “Operator”. Moreover, as part of the rollout of “Operator”, OpenAI has also been partnering with poplar consumer apps to enhance the commercial potency of its platform.

We’re collaborating with companies like DoorDash, Instacart, OpenTable, Priceline, StubHub, Thumbtack, Uber, and others to ensure Operator addresses real-world needs

- OpenAI

It won’t be long until OpenAI expands into offering more sophisticated shopping features as well, given the growing consumer trend of using chatbots to research products.

In fact, AI Search engine provider Perplexity has already expanded into facilitating advanced shopping capabilities, including a partnership with Amazon’s rival Shopify (SHOP).

Similar to OpenAI, the AI Search start-up has also revealed plans to launch its own web browser soon, called “Comet”.

The point is, these upcoming AI rivals are striving to build out their own platforms to accommodate all sorts of commercial activities, posing a challenge to Amazon.

Given the pace at which their user bases are proliferating, it is prudent to expect the e-commerce market to become more fragmented over time, as consumers increasingly start their product searches outside of Amazon.com. It could potentially give individual webstores a better chance to be discovered, undermining Amazon’s ability to hold onto its dominant market share of the e-commerce market.

In 2024, Amazon’s global ecommerce sales alone accounted for 39.7% of all sales from retailers in the Top 2000 [North American Retailers].

– Digital Commerce 360

In an effort to counteract the rise of generative AI-powered search engines and chat assistants, Amazon had introduced its own chatbot “Rufus” in February 2024, which became widely available to all U.S. consumers in September 2024, and has continued to expand to more countries over the past six months.

However, Amazon is still not providing any statistical insights into the consumer usage volumes for Rufus, while upcoming chat assistants like ChatGPT, Gemini and Perplexity already boast daily traffic that is in the millions, as cited earlier.

Moreover, Adobe also offered statistical insights into how consumers are using these new generative AI apps for shopping.

Of the 5,000 individuals surveyed, Adobe found 55% use generative-AI for research, 47% for product recommendations, 43% for seeking deals, 35% for gift ideas and finding unique products, and 33% for creating shopping lists.

- Seeking Alpha News

The fact that consumers are increasingly utilizing these generative AI-powered search tools for product ideas and research poses a significant threat to Amazon, particularly considering the fact that shoppers are not using “Rufus” as much for these use-cases. On Amazon’s last earnings call, CEO Andy Jassy mentioned the kind of queries that shoppers are using “Rufus” for (emphasis added):

If you're buying something and you're on a product detail page, our product detail pages provide so much information that sometimes it's hard if you're trying to find something quickly to scroll through and figure and find that little piece of information. And so we have so many customers now who just use Rufus to help them find a quick fact about a product. They also use Rufus to figure out how to summarize customer reviews, so they [don't] have to read 100 customer reviews to get a sense of what people think about that product.

While these small use-cases like finding “a quick fact” on a “product detail page” and summarizing “customer reviews” can make the shopping experience easier, what we really want to see is consumers coming to Rufus to ask for product ideas, and advice on which product would best suit their needs. Because that is the starting point of a shopping journey, and those are the type of queries that yield valuable insights for more targeted advertising on the Amazon marketplace.

However, “Rufus” is not yet being prominently used in this manner. Instead, consumers are increasingly turning to generative AI-powered Search services and chatbots for product research, which can subsequently send user traffic to non-Amazon retail sites.

This creates a growing risk for Amazon, especially as the use of chat assistants like ChatGPT and Perplexity become deeply imbedded into consumers’ daily habits for all range of tasks, making them a seamless avenue through which they start their shopping journeys too.

Amazon fights back

While new-age AI apps like ChatGPT and Perplexity are continuously advancing their platforms to facilitate more e-commerce activity, Amazon certainly possesses a deep moat that can be difficult to penetrate.

Firstly, Amazon boasts a massive Prime membership base of over 200 million subscribers, bundling benefits such as free & fast shipping with other perks like entertainment through Prime Video/ Music, and Prime Day sales events.

In the United States, industry sources estimated a subscriber count of 180 million in 2024, slightly up from the previous year. That year, roughly 75 percent of all U.S. Amazon shoppers were Prime members, reflecting the service’s strong appeal to platform users.

Prime Day is among the least-important reasons for shoppers to subscribe to Amazon Prime; only 23 percent of U.S. consumers named the sales event as a leading factor. Free shipping and access to Amazon Prime Video were much more sought-after, with roughly 80 and 50 percent of consumers mentioning the two factors.

- Statista (emphasis added)

The e-commerce giant has spent over a decade building up its logistics infrastructure that enables fast delivery in a cost-effective manner. Moreover, Amazon has already been leveraging this strength by introducing its “Buy with Prime” service back in 2022, which essentially allows Prime members to get fast & free delivery on products sold on third-party merchants’ webstores. It is Amazon’s way of staying integrated into consumers’ shopping journeys outside of its own marketplace. The company could continue using this strategy amid the rise of generative AI-powered chat assistants.

Furthermore, interactions with chatbots have mostly been in text-based formats so far. However, as we move onto the agentic phase of this AI revolution, voice-based interactions are expected to become a lot more prevalent. This is where Amazon’s Alexa assistant comes into play, which is imbedded in millions of home devices.

Having passed half a billion devices sold, and with tens of millions of interactions every hour, Alexa has become part of the family in millions of households.

– Amazon

In 2024, Bank of America estimated that “there are roughly 100 million active Alexa users”. Amazon certainly holds a leadership position in home devices.

Although “smartphones” are expected to be a much more important form factor for leveraging generative AI-powered voice assistants, which is where Apple and Google have the lead through iOS and Android, respectively.

Over the past decade, consumers have been increasingly shopping through smartphones, not necessarily their home devices.

Amazon's attempts to convince users to shop through Alexa to generate more revenue have been mostly unsuccessful and the division remains unprofitable.

- Reuters

Nevertheless, the company recently introduced Alexa+, fortified with generative AI capabilities, which is able to facilitate more commercial activities beyond just shopping through Amazon marketplace.

Amazon showed Alexa+ building a grocery shopping list, then ordering items via integrations with Amazon Fresh, Whole Foods, and other local chains. In a separate demo, the company highlighted how Alexa+ can automatically purchase products on Amazon when they go on sale, and reserve spa and fitness appointments through wellness app Vagaro.

The agentic capabilities don’t stop there, according to Amazon. Alexa+ can place food delivery orders through Grubhub, hail an Uber, find tickets to upcoming concerts on Ticketmaster, put together a travel itinerary drawing on sources like Tripadvisor, and even extract key dates and times from an event flyer to set a reminder.

– TechCrunch

Alexa+ will be free for all Prime members, and $19.99 for non-Prime members. It’s unclear how successfully Amazon will be able to convince non-Prime members to pay for Alexa+, given the tight competition from existing players which are already building up loyal user bases for their own premium subscription services.

Below lists the retention rates for the most popular subscription-based chat assistants, acquired from Seeking Alpha News.

I don’t think Amazon’s Alexa+ will necessarily dent the progressive growth of new-age AI applications.

Instead, Amazon’s main opportunity here is to deepen the loyalty of its existing Prime members and Alexa installed base, inducing them to use the new generative AI-powered assistant for various sorts of tasks and commercial activities, instead of alternatives from rivals.

Furthermore, in response to OpenAI’s “Operator” agent discussed earlier, Amazon also introduced its own AI agent “Nova Act” that can take actions within a web browser, which is imbedded into Alexa+.

Alexa+ also introduces agentic capabilities, which will enable Alexa to navigate the internet in a self-directed way to complete tasks on your behalf, behind the scenes. Let’s say you need to get your oven fixed—Alexa+ will be able to navigate the web, use Thumbtack to discover the relevant service provider, authenticate, arrange the repair, and come back to tell you it’s done—there’s no need to supervise or intervene.

– Amazon

So while competitors like OpenAI strive to enhance the commercial potency of their platforms amid growing user bases, Amazon is fighting back by leveraging its existing Alexa installed base.

Although one shortcoming worth highlighting here is that Amazon did not offer evaluation insights for ‘Nova Act’ using industry-recognized testing environments like WebArena and WebVoyager, which would enable performance comparisons with competing agents. On the other hand, OpenAI did disclose the WebVoyager/ WebArena results for its CUA model (which powers the “Operator” agent), while Google DeepMind disclosed the WebVoyager results for “Project Mariner”, its own AI agent for browsers.

The competition in this space is definitely tight.

The investment case for Amazon

Over the past two years, Amazon had been delivering profit margin expansion, particularly on the e-commerce side of the business, with the “North America” segment achieving an 8% operating margin last quarter. This is thanks to continuous cost efficiency measures, including the regionalization of fulfillment centers to store items closer to customers, enabling faster and cheaper delivery.

However, the onset of the trade war will throw a spanner into the works of Amazon to improve its profitability, as the e-commerce giant is likely to absorb most tariff costs to try and keep prices low for customers, at least for the first-party products sold on its marketplace.

It is of no surprise then that the projected EPS FWD Long Term Growth (3-5Y CAGR) for AMZN has been declining over the past few months, from 22.94% in December 2024 to 20.29% now.

I anticipate EPS growth expectations to fall further as Amazon’s executives offer more clarity on how they will be absorbing tariff costs on the upcoming earnings this month.

Nonetheless, AMZN continues to possess one of the highest EPS growth projections out of the Magnificent 7 stocks, behind only Nvidia (NVDA). But again, as Trump’s trade policies become more certain and the market gets better visibility of which tariffs are permanent and which are temporary, Amazon’s EPS growth expectations will be adjusted lower accordingly.

When we adjust each stock’s Forward PE multiple by these anticipated EPS growth rates, we obtain the following Forward Price-Earnings-Growth (Forward PEG) ratios.

For context, a Forward PEG of 1x would implicate that a stock is trading around its fair value.

While AMZN trading at 1.44x is not necessarily expensive, other key AI stocks like GOOG and NVDA are trading at cheaper valuations.

Google is also currently battling the rise of generative AI-powered chat assistants like ChatGPT and Perplexity, undermining the tech giant’s ability to sustain its dominant market share in Search advertising. This uncertainty is primarily why GOOG is trading at 1.08x Forward PEG.

Similar to Google’s plight, Amazon is also at risk of people starting their shopping journeys through new-age AI apps instead of the search engine on its marketplace. This could undermine the company’s e-commerce sales revenue, as well as it advertising revenue going forward.

While the nascency of apps like ChatGPT may not seem like a direct threat at present, they are gradually cultivating into full-blown platforms facilitating various forms of commercial activities. Their rise could undermine Amazon’s e-commerce market share, especially in international markets where people are less likely to be Prime members and Alexa users, and where Amazon’s delivery infrastructure is less developed relative to North America. Hence, Amazon investors should certainly keep the growth of these AI apps under their radars.

Amazon stock is now rated a “hold”.

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

data.title: Victory Capital: Screaming Hold Wherever You Look
data.description:
Victory Capital (NASDAQ:VCTR), a diversified multi-boutique, global asset management firm, appears to be doing well financially and is definitely a fundamentally sound investment. I see it as a cash generating business of long term strategic value. However, wherever I see, from balance sheets to share prices, I see stagnation. Also, short-term catalysts are worrying.

Macro will Hurt Fees and AUM

Expect markets to be volatile and upside capped unless something changes drastically on the trade war and economic stagnation front. The short-term damage, set in already, is sufficient to drag net inflows into asset management firms across the board. Additionally, fee compression threats will come into play.

AUM impacts one multiplier of the revenue and there VCTR has been a story of net outflows for a few quarters now. After a brief acute redemption pressure in 4Q 2022, the AUM continued to recover (though still net outflow). Since Q2 2024, net outflow has increased again. The market volatility in Q1 2025 will not be too kind on this metric and that is likely to continue for a few quarters.

VCTR has also already implemented expense waivers and reimbursements for certain funds. Zero commission fees on trading is also likely to be a continuing pressure if trader interest wanes - in a market already pressed for lower trading costs amid widespread competition. Fee structures are the other important multiplier in the revenue outcome.

While overall revenue has not suffered significantly in the past few quarters, it has had blips and stagnation in the past 4 years as a result of these two strong segment trends - something that can only get worse before getting any better.

A Look at Developments and Numbers

VCTR has been trying to reignite the growth spark through partnerships, acquisitions, and platform improvements. The last game-changing acquisition, a rerating trigger, was the acquisition of USAA Asset Management Company in 2019, which gave VCTR access to the US military retirement scheme. This changed the VCTR brand perception, in addition to a foothold in a sticky client base. Nothing visible in terms of strategy or industry trends supports such an explosion as of now.

Partnership with Amundi, expected to close by Q1 2025, will drive cost synergies. The ETF platform and digital reach has been significant over many years now. Interest Expenses, which showed signs of decrease in Q4 2024, will likely continue to benefit if the interest rate regime remains docile or further rate cuts happen.

Overall expenses too have remained within range - no negative surprises there. However, as Revenues have stagnated, a rangebound cost control environment has meant rangebound gross and operating margins. Overall, the story has been one of strong financial numbers, nothing to complain about, but screams stagnation whichever way you look at it. While strategic acquisitions and partnerships have helped drive cost synergies, platform improvements and improved offerings, VCTR looks like waiting for an explosion moment on top line as well as bottom line and the current situation definitely does not place it well for that.

Valuation Neither Here Nor There

With a forward PE of 9.22, VCTR is valued higher than its 5-year average, which is surprising, given the headline stagnation evident everywhere in its business. Valuation is reasonable compared to the sector median, but it's not a buy magnet on valuation alone. EV/EBITDA looks a lot cheaper, though, both in comparison to its own historical average as well as peers, but is fair given VCTR’s limited pricing power.

Price action also does not show any attractive corrections to demand a buy at this point. It appears like most of the financial soundness and existing business’ positives are well priced in.

The Seeking Alpha Quant Rating supports the bland view as well, with a Hold rating on the basis of growth and profitability on one hand, but neutral valuation and momentum indicators on the other.

Risks to the Thesis

While a lot of assumptions are based on declining inflows as the market corrects, we know how the last correction panned out for asset managers in 2020. Volatility may trigger more rebalancing, more investments on corrections, movement of funds between active and passive strategies depending on market structure. Volatile or bear markets are sometimes the best times that force product innovations, and maybe that is the trigger that catapults VCTR to the move we are waiting for. Of course, current market conditions are way different from the demand induced short-term market impacts in 2020. I believe this time, VCTR will give better entry point opportunity to fresh investors, even if it rebounds stronger.

Hold Victory Capital

The thesis is consistently a "Hold" call on Victory Capital from all counts. Existing investors will likely note the stable free cash flow, unlikely to dent chances of downgrades in dividend payouts, even if the business gets impacted in the next few quarters by global headwinds. While existing investors will find the existing yield of 3.37% (likely to continue) a cushion after having ridden the growth wave of the stock price appreciation, fresh investors may not find the yield compensating enough for the lack of growth prospects going ahead (and even an imminent threat of business drops). Existing investors can also consider booking partial profits depending on when they entered the VCTR journey.
